description.s: Potential use cases for stress-testing and showcasing Hobson's architecture. Each suggestion is assessed for which principles and subsystems it exercises. Grouped into internal capabilities (building within Hobson's own primitives) and external data import (bringing outside data into Hobson).
Several directions here overlap with existing idea items. Where an existing item covers similar ground, it's noted inline.
These directions build new functionality entirely from Hobson primitives — types, views, scripts, watches, and the existing kernel.
type with amount, date, category (tag or item reference), and account references. A ledger view queries all transactions for an account and renders a running balance.

Views that query across items and present derived/aggregated data rather than rendering a single item's content. Forces the question of whether view-specs can express tabular layouts with computed columns. Demands a "table view" that doesn't yet exist.
view that queries all tasks, groups them by status into columns, and allows drag-to-change-status.

Cross-item views (a single view rendering many items it doesn't "own" as children). Declarative watches for enforcing transition rules or auto-assigning timestamps on status changes. Spatial interaction qualitatively different from the free-form container.
Interactive (each drag suggests the next action), continuous, immediate.
items that capture what changed, when, and a diff of previous vs new state. A timeline view browses these entries.

Watch system at scale (every save triggers a watcher). Whether watchers creating items triggers further watchers (infinite loop prevention). Self-reference — the system observing its own mutation stream and recording it as data within itself.
Alive with data, inspectable, always on.

High architectural insight, but the effort is easily underestimated. Cycle-breaking (watches that create items triggering further watches) is prerequisite infrastructure that doesn't exist yet. This direction is worth pursuing
the watch system has explicit loop-prevention semantics. Don't start here unless you want to build that first.
type whose children alternate between markdown note items and executable script items, rendered in linear sequence by a dedicated notebook view. Running a script cell writes output into the item's

Compositional rendering (a view rendering a sequence of heterogeneous children, each with their own sub-view). The script execution model. Whether script outputs should themselves be items (radical uniformity would say yes).
Symmetrical (building the tool with the tool while using the tool), unified, alive with data.
view that lets you visually construct a type definition and its view-spec simultaneously, with a live preview of what an instance would look like as you add fields and choose field views.

Self-reference — using Hobson's view system to build new entries in Hobson's type system. Highlights friction in the current type/view-spec workflow. Whether the system can be truly self-revealing rather than requiring REPL/JSON knowledge.
Self-revealing (the most underexercised of the Humane Dozen in current implementation), interactive, immediate.

Highest priority among internal capabilities. Right now creating types requires JSON editing or REPL knowledge, making the system opaque to anyone who doesn't already understand its internals. This is the single biggest gap between Hobson's philosophy and its reality. A visual type designer would make the system genuinely self-revealing.
type, where meetings reference contacts via item references. A contact's view shows a reverse-lookup of all meetings mentioning that contact.

The relationship model — forces the question of whether Hobson can efficiently answer "which items reference this item?" (reverse queries). Tests whether the same item can appear meaningfully in multiple contexts (a contact's history, a project timeline, a weekly review). Natural testing ground for explicit relationship items.
Reverse-reference queries for this are already handled by the existing
, which maintains an incremental inverse index across types, tags, parents, containers,
A view that renders the entire item graph visually — items as nodes, type chains and child references as edges, with click-to-inspect/navigate on any node.

Rendering at scale (hundreds of items). Self-awareness — the graph explorer is an item rendering items that include itself. Would reveal orphaned items, broken type chains, or unexpectedly deep hierarchies.
Visually compelling but unclear practical value beyond the initial "wow" — the

idea item itself raises this concern. Force-directed graphs of hundreds of items tend to become hairballs. For structured navigation and debugging, the System Browser (1.13) is more practically useful. The graph explorer remains interesting as a spatial overview or for spotting structural anomalies (orphans, clusters), but it's a complement to the browser, not a substitute.
type and a watcher or script that auto-creates today's entry from a template when navigating to the journal container and no entry exists for today. The template itself is an item.

Temporal patterns (date-based queries, "does today's item exist?"). Template instantiation (copying structure from one item to another). The gap between passive storage and active/proactive behaviour.
Rendering an item inline within another item's view — like Obsidian's
syntax but generalized. A note could transclude a table, a chart, or another note. The transcluded item remains a live, editable instance, not a copy.

Recursive view nesting — a view rendering a sub-view for an item it doesn't own. Cycle detection (item A transcludes B which transcludes A). Whether the rendering system can handle arbitrary nesting depth without performance degradation. The boundary between "attachment" (owned child) and "reference" (linked peer).
which invokes an export and renders the returned DOM), and query blocks (
helpers). The markdown editor has an "Insert Link/Transclusion" picker.

— transclusions are static snapshots rendered at display time. If the source item changes, the transclusion doesn't refresh until the containing item re-renders. A watch-based approach could re-render transclusions when their source items emit
— transcluded content is read-only. Editing in place (rather than navigating to the source) would complete the "live instance, not a copy" vision.
— not currently handled. If item A transcludes B which transcludes A, the renderer will recurse until it hits a stack limit.

Items whose content fields are derived from other items' fields, forming a reactive pipeline. Item A contains raw data; item B has a transform script that reads A's output and produces a filtered/aggregated result; item C visualizes B's output. Changes to A propagate through B to C automatically.

Whether the watch/event system can express dataflow dependencies, not just mutation observers. Topological ordering of updates. The difference between "react to a change" (current watches) and "this value is defined as a function of that value" (reactive bindings). Circular dependency detection.

This is a more radical architectural direction. The current watch system is event-driven (imperative: "when X changes, do Y"). Reactive dataflow is declarative ("Z is always f(X, Y)"). Both models are useful; the question is whether Hobson needs the latter or can simulate it adequately with the former.

A mechanism for packaging a coherent set of related items (type-def + view-spec + view + library + sample data) into an exportable bundle that another Hobson instance can import. The bundle is itself an item.

Whether items are truly portable. GUID collision handling on import. Dependency resolution (does this bundle require a library that the target instance doesn't have?). The gap between "everything is an item" and "I can share my setup with someone else."

Not urgent, but important for Hobson's long-term viability beyond a single-user system. Every direction in Part 2 produces a set of items (importer script + type + view) that would benefit from being shareable as a bundle.

A command palette (Ctrl+K or similar) that searches across items, actions, and scripts. Keyboard bindings are themselves items mapping key combos to script items. The palette view is an item. Users can add custom commands by creating script items and binding them.

Whether Hobson's interaction model can go beyond mouse-driven spatial manipulation. Script items as actions (not just background processes). The tension between a live spatial environment and the efficiency of keyboard-driven workflows that power tools like Emacs, Vim, and Raycast.
Hobson already has Ctrl+K search. This direction extends it into a full command system where the commands themselves are items.

A multi-pane hierarchical browser for navigating the item system by structure rather than by search or spatial layout. Start from an organizational axis (all types, all tags) and drill down: pick a type → see its instances → pick an instance → see its relationships and content. Inspired by Smalltalk's System Browser, which was the primary way developers understood, navigated, and edited a live Smalltalk image.
— the existing relationship inspector that would share infrastructure with a browser.
indexes are sufficient to power structured navigation (they likely are —
already answer the core queries). The difference between showing relationships for one item (what the widget does) and providing drill-down navigation across the whole system.
High priority relative to effort. The query engine already exists in

. The related-items widget already demonstrates reactive rendering from those indexes. A System Browser is essentially a new view that presents the same data as columnar drill-down rather than a flat relationship list. This addresses the "self-revealing" gap alongside the Type Workshop: the Workshop solves

— what types exist, what fields they have, what views are available, what instances exist. Building the Browser first would also provide useful reference browsing while designing types in the Workshop.

These directions bring data from outside sources into Hobson for offline use. The common pattern: the importer is a script item, the imported data is typed items, and the views are view items. Every layer is inspectable, modifiable, and part of the same graph.
A script item that takes a URL or pasted HTML, extracts article content (title, body, author, date), and creates a

item. A reading view displays clippings. Highlights and margin notes are child items — they appear in search, can be tagged, and can be referenced from other notes. The clipping script is editable for tweaking extraction logic per site.
Content extraction and normalisation. Child items as annotations. The knowledge graph absorbing external content.

type storing URL, title, description, and tags. A script fetches the page and stores a plain-text or markdown snapshot in the item's content. Over time the collection becomes a searchable personal archive. Tags + the existing tag browser provide faceted navigation for free.
Lightweight import pattern. Whether existing views (tag browser, search) handle a growing item count gracefully.
type (storing the feed URL) and a

type. A script fetches XML, parses it, and creates/updates feed-entry items. Entries are viewable in list view or a custom reader view. Because entries are items, they can be tagged, linked to project notes, or dragged into containers. The feed script tracks
Bulk item creation from structured external data. Incremental import (update without duplication). Everyday utility as a real tool.
type storing an API endpoint, headers, and a transform script (all in
). Running the fetch script pulls JSON and creates/updates
items. Views query these items and render charts or tables. Applicable to weather forecasts, exchange rates, smart meter data, GitHub stats — anything with a JSON API.
The transform logic is a code item editable live. Data points are inspectable individual items. Dashboard views are forkable and modifiable. It's a personal Grafana where every layer is transparent.

script that reads a file, infers fields, creates a type-definition item, creates a view-spec for that type, and then creates one item per row. Applicable to recipes, plant species, film ratings, vocabulary lists — any tabular data.
Produces hundreds of items at once. Forces confrontation with how views and search perform at scale. Auto-generation of types and view-specs from data.
Import an mbox or JSON email dump. A script creates

items (from, to, date, subject, body, thread-id). Thread-id provides grouping. The email archive becomes part of the knowledge graph — emails linkable to project notes, conversations taggable, search spanning emails and notes simultaneously.
Radical uniformity — an email and a note are peers, differing only in type and view. Volume (potentially thousands of items). Thread-based grouping as a relationship pattern.
type defining a URL pattern, CSS selectors for extraction, and a target type. Running it crawls pages and creates typed items from extracted data. Recipes from cooking sites become
A personal structured mirror of parts of the web, entirely offline, searchable, and linkable. The scrape job itself is an inspectable, modifiable, re-runnable item.
Fetch a repo's tree via the GitHub API or a tarball and represent it in Hobson: a
items with syntax-highlighted code views. Annotate source files with child note items. Link functions to design documents. Tag files by subsystem.
Code review as a spatial, personal activity. Deep container nesting. Using Hobson for what Smalltalk's system browser did, but for external codebases.
Export a calendar as ICS, import events as

items with start, end, location, attendees (as item references to contacts). Build a timeline or agenda view. The payoff: events become linkable from everything else. Meeting notes reference event items. Project containers include milestone events. Weekly review queries pull in notes and meetings together.
Time as a first-class dimension of the knowledge graph. Cross-type queries (events + notes + projects). Item references between imported and hand-created data.

), podcast show notes (via RSS), bookmarked tweets (via archive JSON), annotated PDF passages. Each source has its own importer script. Everything lands as appropriately typed items with source attribution. Tag and link freely across sources.
The Obenauer vision at its most potent — an entire intellectual input stream unified in one graph. Multiple importer scripts coexisting. Cross-source discovery through tags and search.
Aspirational. Each source is really its own sub-project. Valuable as a north-star vision but not a single deliverable.
Import data from fitness trackers (Fitbit/Garmin JSON exports), sleep apps, mood journals, habit trackers. Types for
. Views rendering sparklines or heatmaps from queried items. Link a bad sleep night to a journal entry. Tag a run with the project you were mulling over.

Numeric/temporal data at volume. Visualisation views (charts, heatmaps) that don't exist yet. The "alive with data" principle taken literally — life data flowing through the same system as thoughts about it.
Aspirational, like 2.10. Each data source and visualisation type is its own sub-project.
Revised priority order, informed by dependency analysis:
Reverse-reference queries — often a prerequisite for relationship-heavy features — are already solved by
, which maintains an incremental inverse index and is surfaced by the
— Highest impact. Directly closes the "self-revealing" gap, which is the most underexercised Humane Dozen principle. Makes every subsequent direction easier because new types can be created visually.
— High value relative to effort. The query engine (
half of the self-revealing gap (what types exist? what views? what instances?) while the Type Workshop solves
. Provides useful reference browsing for every subsequent direction.
— High architectural insight, but requires watch-system cycle prevention as a prerequisite. Sequence this after the watch system has loop-breaking semantics.
— The simplest external data pattern that's genuinely useful day-to-day. Good proof that the import pattern works.
— Stress-tests scale and auto-generation of types/views from data. Pairs well with the Type Workshop.

— The core transclusion primitive is already implemented (full, partial, functional, query blocks). What remains is reactive updates (transclusions refreshing when source items change), inline editing, and cycle detection. These are incremental improvements, not a new feature.
— Interesting as a spatial overview and for spotting structural anomalies, but the System Browser covers the practical navigation need. Lower priority as a complement rather than a prerequisite.
